CHSY1 Deficiency and Reduced Chondroitin Sulfate Biosynthesis
Dysregulated BMP-CHSY1 Limb and Inner-Ear Patterning

nodes.description: Convergent dysregulation of NOTCH and BMP signaling, downstream of reduced chondroitin sulfate biosynthesis, produces abnormal limb and skeletal patterning and ossification. The result is the characteristic preaxial brachydactyly with hyperphalangism and partial duplication/splitting of proximal phalanges in digits 1-3, symphalangism, and carpal/tarsal and radioulnar synostoses, together with craniofacial and inner-ear anomalies.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of CHSY1, a secreted FRINGE enzyme, causes syndromic brachydactyly in humans via increased NOTCH signaling.
evidence.snippet: In vivo, chsy1 knockdown in zebrafish embryos partially phenocopied the human disorder; it increased NOTCH output and impaired skeletal, pectoral-fin, and retinal development.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of CHSY1, a secreted FRINGE enzyme, causes syndromic brachydactyly in humans via increased NOTCH signaling.
evidence.snippet: We conclude that CHSY1 is a secreted FRINGE enzyme required for adjustment of NOTCH signaling throughout human and fish embryogenesis and particularly during limb patterning.

nodes.description: CHSY1 is a secreted glycosyltransferase, bearing a Fringe domain, that is required for the synthesis of chondroitin sulfate chains on proteoglycans. Biallelic loss-of-function mutations eliminate CHSY1 activity, reducing chondroitin sulfate moieties and altering the extracellular matrix and proteoglycan context that supports cartilage, bone, and craniofacial development. TPBS is therefore part of the spectrum of disorders caused by defects in glycosaminoglycan/proteoglycan biosynthesis.
nodes.id: CHSY1 Deficiency and Reduced Chondroitin Sulfate Biosynthesis

evidence.reference_title: Loss of CHSY1, a secreted FRINGE enzyme, causes syndromic brachydactyly in humans via increased NOTCH signaling.
evidence.snippet: CHSY1 was secreted from patients' fibroblasts and was required for synthesis of chondroitin sulfate moieties.

evidence.reference_title: Temtamy preaxial brachydactyly syndrome is caused by loss-of-function mutations in chondroitin synthase 1, a potential target of BMP signaling.
evidence.snippet: We show that loss of human CHSY1 function causes autosomal-recessive Temtamy preaxial brachydactyly syndrome (TPBS), mainly characterized by limb malformations, short stature, and hearing loss.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of CHSY1, a secreted FRINGE enzyme, causes syndromic brachydactyly in humans via increased NOTCH signaling.
evidence.snippet: High-throughput sequencing of all 177 candidate genes detected a truncating frameshift mutation in the gene CHSY1 encoding a chondroitin synthase with a Fringe domain.

evidence.reference_title: A novel CHSY1 gene mutation underlies Temtamy preaxial brachydactyly syndrome in a Pakistani family.
evidence.snippet: The mutation predicted substitution of a highly conserved aspartate amino acid residue to asparagine at position 633 in the protein (D633N).

nodes.description: CHSY1 has been identified as a potential mediator/target of BMP signaling. In zebrafish, unrestricted Bmp2b signaling or loss of the BMP inhibitor Dan reduces chsy1 expression, and Chsy1 perturbation produces inner-ear and other developmental defects similar to those seen with altered BMP signaling. Both loss and gain of chsy1 produce comparable zebrafish phenotypes, paralleling how human brachydactyly can result from either reduced or excessive BMP signaling. This BMP-CHSY1 axis links the chondroitin sulfate defect to limb, craniofacial, and inner-ear (hearing) phenotypes.
nodes.id: Dysregulated BMP-CHSY1 Limb and Inner-Ear Patterning

evidence.reference_title: Temtamy preaxial brachydactyly syndrome is caused by loss-of-function mutations in chondroitin synthase 1, a potential target of BMP signaling.
evidence.snippet: Furthermore, unrestricted Bmp2b signaling or loss of Dan activity leads to reduced chsy1 expression and, during epithelial morphogenesis, defects similar to those that occur upon Chsy1 inactivation, indicating that Bmp signaling affects inner-ear development by repressing chsy1.

evidence.reference_title: Temtamy preaxial brachydactyly syndrome is caused by loss-of-function mutations in chondroitin synthase 1, a potential target of BMP signaling.
evidence.snippet: In addition, we obtained strikingly similar zebrafish phenotypes after chsy1 overexpression, which might explain why, in humans, brachydactyly can be caused by mutations leading either to loss or to gain of BMP signaling.

nodes.description: Sensorineural hearing loss in TPBS is managed supportively with audiologic rehabilitation, including hearing aids.

evidence.reference_title: Temtamy preaxial brachydactyly syndrome is caused by loss-of-function mutations in chondroitin synthase 1, a potential target of BMP signaling.
evidence.snippet: We show that loss of human CHSY1 function causes autosomal-recessive Temtamy preaxial brachydactyly syndrome (TPBS), mainly characterized by limb malformations, short stature, and hearing loss.

nodes.description: Loss of CHSY1 triggers massive production of the Notch ligand JAG1 and subsequent activation of NOTCH signaling. This effect requires CHSY1's catalytic Fringe activity, as it is reversed by wild-type but not a catalytically dead CHSY1 construct. Dysregulated NOTCH output disturbs skeletal patterning, particularly during limb development, and CHSY1 depletion also enhances osteogenesis in fetal osteoblasts and upregulates JAG2 in other cell types.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of CHSY1, a secreted FRINGE enzyme, causes syndromic brachydactyly in humans via increased NOTCH signaling.
evidence.snippet: Noticeably, its absence triggered massive production of JAG1 and subsequent NOTCH activation, which could only be reversed with a wild-type but not a Fringe catalytically dead CHSY1 construct.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of CHSY1, a secreted FRINGE enzyme, causes syndromic brachydactyly in humans via increased NOTCH signaling.
evidence.snippet: In vitro, depletion of CHSY1 by RNAi knockdown resulted in enhanced osteogenesis in fetal osteoblasts and remarkable upregulation of JAG2 in glioblastoma cells.

nodes.description: Limb malformations including the digital anomalies and synostoses may be addressed with orthopedic/hand surgical procedures as part of multidisciplinary supportive management.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of CHSY1, a secreted FRINGE enzyme, causes syndromic brachydactyly in humans via increased NOTCH signaling.
evidence.snippet: We delineated a syndromic recessive preaxial brachydactyly with partial duplication of proximal phalanges to 16.8 Mb over 4 chromosomes.
